title: Visual Analytics Group Project
text:
This project is based on Mini-Challenge 1 of VAST Challenge 2026.
TenantThread is a property technology company whose automated communication system released embargoed merger information before the official announcement deadline. The case focuses on Project HarborCrest, a confidential merger agreement between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners.
Through this project, we develop a visual analytics application to explore the communication patterns, agent behaviours, and key events leading up to the embargo breach.
For the detailed project objectives and proposed approach, please refer to the Proposal section.

title: Statistical Analysis
text:
This section outlines the analytical methods used to investigate the TenantThread embargo breach in VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1.
The investigation combines temporal, behavioural, communication, and network analysis to identify how the embargoed information may have moved through the system. No single measure is treated as proof of responsibility. Instead, findings are interpreted together with the timing of events, communication content, communication channels, and the roles of involved agents.

title: Statistical Analysis
text:
1.1 Investigation windows
The inappropriate release occurred on 5 June 2046. To identify whether activity changed before the breach, communications are compared across three periods:

Normal period: More than five days before the breach.
Escalation period: Three to five days before the breach.
Critical period: Zero to two days before the breach.

This comparison helps distinguish routine communication behaviour from unusual activity immediately before the inappropriate release.

title: Statistical Analysis
text:
1.2 Event-sequence reconstruction
Communications are examined in chronological order alongside relevant alerts, deadlines, market events, agent availability, and other contextual events.
This analysis identifies:

important events that created urgency or pressure;
communications relating to embargo controls, approvals, compliance, or publication;
important decisions and actors involved in those decisions; and
the sequence through which sensitive information may have reached a higher-exposure communication channel.

title: Statistical Analysis
text:
2.1 Message volume and recipient reach
Communication activity is analysed by comparing:

total number of messages;
number of unique recipients;
communication frequency by agent and role; and
changes in activity across the normal, escalation, and critical periods.

A sudden increase in message volume or recipient reach may indicate heightened coordination or information dissemination before the breach.

title: Statistical Analysis
text:
2.2 Communication-channel exposure
Messages are classified based on their communication channel:

Internal group channels represent controlled internal discussion.
Private channels represent one-to-one communication.
Side-channel communication represents smaller and less visible discussion spaces.
Public channels represent communications with the greatest potential for wider dissemination.

This analysis helps identify whether information moved from controlled communication spaces towards more exposed channels before the embargo breach.

title: Statistical Analysis
Statistical Analysis
Statistical Analysis
text:
5.1 Weighted degree centrality
Weighted out-degree centrality identifies agents who send messages frequently or communicate with many recipients. These agents may act as important information distributors.
Weighted in-degree centrality identifies agents who receive messages from many others. These agents may act as coordination points, decision recipients, or key information recipients.

title: Statistical Analysis
text:
5.2 PageRank centrality
PageRank identifies agents who are prominent within the wider communication network.
An agent with a high PageRank score is connected to other well-connected agents and may occupy an important position in the movement of information. This does not indicate responsibility for the breach, but it helps identify agents whose communication position warrants closer examination.

title: Statistical Analysis
text:
5.3 Betweenness centrality
Betweenness centrality identifies agents positioned between otherwise separate communication groups.
These agents may act as communication brokers or bridges between functions such as Legal, PR, Social Media, and Platform Trust. This is particularly relevant when an agent connects a controlled internal discussion with a public-facing or publishing-related pathway.

title: Data Preparation
text: This page documents how the VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1 data, stored as a nested RDS object, is converted into clean, reusable tables for the investigation and the Shiny application. The RDS object was previously created from the supplied JSON data. This page focuses only on data loading, validation and feature engineering; no charts are produced here.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Loading packages
The following packages are used to load and standardise the nested RDS source, prepare tabular data, derive time-based and case-specific features, and create network-ready data tables.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Loading the raw MC1 data
The supplied Mini-Challenge 1 dataset has already been converted from JSON into an RDS object named mc1_data.rds. It contains a sequence of simulation rounds, each with participants, communication records and environmental context. The RDS object is loaded using a relative path so that the project can be rendered on another group member’s computer without changing the code.


Code
# This QMD file is stored in the Methodology folder.
# Therefore, the source data is referenced relative to this folder.
data_path <- file.path("data", "MC1", "mc1_data.rds")

if (!file.exists(data_path)) {
  stop(
    "Dataset not found. Please save mc1_data.rds in: ",
    normalizePath(file.path("data", "MC1"), winslash = "/", mustWork = FALSE)
  )
}

# Load the nested RDS object that was previously created from the MC1 JSON file.
mc1_rds <- readr::read_rds(data_path)

if (!is.list(mc1_rds) || is.null(mc1_rds$rounds)) {
  stop(
    "mc1_data.rds was loaded, but it does not contain the expected $rounds element. ",
    "Please ensure that this is the MC1 RDS file created from MC1_final_00.json."
  )
}

# The source RDS stores deeply nested lists. This standardisation step keeps the
# existing unnesting code below compatible with the RDS version of the data.
mc1_data <- jsonlite::fromJSON(
  jsonlite::toJSON(mc1_rds, auto_unbox = TRUE, null = "null"),
  simplifyVector = TRUE
)

df_rounds_raw <- tibble::as_tibble(mc1_data$rounds)

round_summary <- tibble(
  Measure = c("Number of simulation rounds", "Fields in each round"),
  Value = c(nrow(df_rounds_raw), ncol(df_rounds_raw))
)

title: Data Preparation
text:
Communications
Communication records are unpacked at message level. Internal state and agent-round metadata are retained because they may later support behavioural and governance analysis.


Code
df_communications <- df_rounds_raw %>%
  select(hour, communications) %>%
  unnest(communications) %>%
  unpack(any_of(c("internal_state", "agent_round_metadata")), names_sep = "_")

normalise_recipients <- function(x) {
  if (is.null(x) || length(x) == 0 || all(is.na(x))) {
    return(NA_character_)
  }

  stringr::str_c(as.character(unlist(x, use.names = FALSE)), collapse = ", ")
}

communications <- df_communications %>%
  mutate(
    recipients = purrr::map_chr(recipients, normalise_recipients)
  ) %>%
  rename(round_hour = hour)

knitr::kable(
  communications %>%
    select(any_of(c(
      "round_hour", "message_id", "agent_id", "agent_role", "channel",
      "message_type", "recipients", "content", "timestamp"
    ))) %>%
    head(10)
)

title: Data Preparation
text:
Environmental context
The scalar fields in the environmental context, including the market snapshot, are retained in one table. Nested event arrays are extracted separately because a single simulation round can contain several events.


Code
df_environment <- df_rounds_raw %>%
  select(hour, environment_context) %>%
  unpack(environment_context, names_sep = "_") %>%
  unpack(any_of("environment_context_market_snapshot"), names_sep = "_") %>%
  select(where(~ !is.list(.)))

# Derive clean fields where the corresponding source columns are available.
df_environment_clean <- df_environment %>%
  mutate(hour_datetime = lubridate::ymd_hms(hour, quiet = TRUE))

if ("environment_context_market_snapshot_stock_price" %in% names(df_environment_clean)) {
  df_environment_clean <- df_environment_clean %>%
    mutate(
      stock_price_numeric = readr::parse_number(
        as.character(environment_context_market_snapshot_stock_price)
      )
    )
}

if ("environment_context_market_snapshot_percent_change" %in% names(df_environment_clean)) {
  df_environment_clean <- df_environment_clean %>%
    mutate(
      stock_percent_numeric = readr::parse_number(

title: Data Preparation
text:
Environmental event tables
The helper function below extracts each nested environment array and keeps the hour field as the key for joining events back to a simulation round.


Code
extract_environment_array <- function(rounds, array_column) {

  environment_data <- rounds %>%
    select(hour, environment_context) %>%
    unpack(environment_context, names_sep = "_")

  if (!array_column %in% names(environment_data)) {
    return(tibble(hour = character()))
  }

  environment_data %>%
    select(hour, all_of(array_column)) %>%
    unnest(all_of(array_column), keep_empty = FALSE)
}

df_media_events <- extract_environment_array(
  df_rounds_raw,
  "environment_context_media_events"
)

df_external_actions <- extract_environment_array(
  df_rounds_raw,
  "environment_context_external_actor_actions"
)

df_social_alerts <- extract_environment_array(
  df_rounds_raw,
  "environment_context_social_manager_alerts"
)

df_agents_unavailable <- extract_environment_array(
  df_rounds_raw,
  "environment_context_agents_unavailable"
)

df_critical_deadlines <- extract_environment_array(
  df_rounds_raw,
  "environment_context_critical_deadlines"
)

df_news <- extract_environment_array(
  df_rounds_raw,
  "environment_context_news"
)

title: Data Preparation
text:
Temporal features
The inappropriate release occurred on 5 June 2046. The features below distinguish normal activity from the escalation and critical windows immediately before the breach.


Code
breach_date <- lubridate::ymd("2046-06-05")

communications_clean <- communications %>%
  mutate(
    timestamp = lubridate::ymd_hms(timestamp, quiet = TRUE),
    round_hour = lubridate::ymd_hms(round_hour, quiet = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(timestamp) %>%
  mutate(
    communication_date = as_date(timestamp),
    communication_hour = hour(timestamp),
    communication_day = wday(timestamp, label = TRUE),

    communication_period = case_when(
      communication_hour >= 6 & communication_hour < 12 ~ "Morning",
      communication_hour >= 12 & communication_hour < 18 ~ "Afternoon",
      communication_hour >= 18 & communication_hour < 24 ~ "Evening",
      TRUE ~ "Late Night"
    ),

    days_before_embargo_breach = as.integer(breach_date - communication_date),

    near_breach_window = case_when(
      days_before_embargo_breach < 0 ~ "Post-breach",
      days_before_embargo_breach <= 2 ~ "Critical Period",
      days_before_embargo_breach <= 5 ~ "Escalation Period",
      TRUE ~ "Normal Period"
    ),

    message_sequence = row_number()
  )

knitr::kable(
  communications_clean %>%
    select(
      timestamp, communication_date, communication_hour,
      communication_period, days_before_embargo_breach,
      near_breach_window, message_sequence
    ) %>%
    head(10)
)

title: Data Preparation
text:
Communication scope and exposure risk
Channels are grouped into broader communication scopes. This allows later analyses to distinguish public communication from private, internal and side-channel exchanges.


Code
communications_clean <- communications_clean %>%
  mutate(
    communication_scope = case_when(
      channel %in% c("official_post", "personal_post") ~ "Public",
      channel %in% c("one_on_one_chat") ~ "Private",
      channel %in% c("side_huddle") ~ "Side-channel",
      channel %in% c("comms_huddle") ~ "Internal Group",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),

    exposure_risk_level = case_when(
      communication_scope == "Public" ~ "High Exposure",
      communication_scope == "Side-channel" ~ "Moderate Exposure",
      communication_scope == "Private" ~ "Low Exposure",
      communication_scope == "Internal Group" ~ "Controlled Exposure",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    ),

    is_public_channel = communication_scope == "Public"
  )

channel_summary <- communications_clean %>%
  count(channel, communication_scope, exposure_risk_level, sort = TRUE)

title: Data Preparation
text:
Text and behavioural indicators
Message content and any available internal-state text are combined into one analysis field. Keyword indicators then identify potential references to the merger, embargo controls, public disclosure and operational pressure.


Code
combine_text_fields <- function(data, columns) {
  if (length(columns) == 0) {
    return(rep("", nrow(data)))
  }

  apply(data[columns], 1, function(x) {
    stringr::str_squish(
      stringr::str_c(replace_na(as.character(x), ""), collapse = " ")
    )
  })
}

internal_state_columns <- names(communications_clean) %>%
  stringr::str_subset("^internal_state_")

communications_clean$internal_state_text <- combine_text_fields(
  communications_clean,
  internal_state_columns
)

communications_clean <- communications_clean %>%
  mutate(
    content_lower = str_to_lower(replace_na(content, "")),
    analysis_text = str_squish(
      str_c(content_lower, str_to_lower(internal_state_text), sep = " ")
    ),

    contains_project_terms = str_detect(
      analysis_text,
      "harborcrest|civicloom|merger|rebrand|capital|deal"
    ),

    contains_embargo_terms = str_detect(
      analysis_text,
      "embargo|judge|compliance|approved|blocked|deadline"
    ),

    contains_public_exposure_terms = str_detect(
      analysis_text,
      "public|post|fle?x|release|leak|disclose|announcement"
    ),

    contains_pressure_terms = str_detect(
      analysis_text,
      "pressure|urgent|concern|risk|legal|scrutiny|journal|algorithmiceviction"
    ),

    has_internal_reasoning = str_length(internal_state_text) > 0,
    message_length = str_length(content_lower),
    word_count = str_count(content_lower, "\\S+"),

    risk_signal_score =
      as.integer(contains_project_terms) +
      as.integer(contains_embargo_terms) +
      as.integer(contains_public_exposure_terms) +
      as.integer(contains_pressure_terms),

    risk_signal_group = case_when(
      risk_signal_score >= 3 ~ "High Signal",
      risk_signal_score == 2 ~ "Moderate Signal",
      risk_signal_score == 1 ~ "Low Signal",
      TRUE ~ "No Signal"
    )
  )

risk_signal_summary <- communications_clean %>%
  count(near_breach_window, risk_signal_group, sort = TRUE)

text:
Nodes and weighted edges
Nodes represent all unique senders and recipients. Weighted edges aggregate repeated sender-recipient interactions and retain communication scope and risk classification.


Code
communication_nodes <- tibble(
  name = sort(unique(c(communication_edges$from, communication_edges$to)))
) %>%
  left_join(
    communications_clean %>%
      distinct(agent_id, agent_role) %>%
      rename(name = agent_id, role = agent_role),
    by = "name"
  ) %>%
  mutate(role = replace_na(role, "Recipient / System"))

communication_edges_weighted <- communication_edges %>%
  count(
    from,
    to,
    communication_scope,
    exposure_risk_level,
    risk_signal_group,
    name = "weight"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    interaction_intensity = case_when(
      weight >= 10 ~ "High Interaction",
      weight >= 5 ~ "Moderate Interaction",
      TRUE ~ "Low Interaction"
    )
  )

network_inventory <- tibble(
  Measure = c(
    "Communication nodes",
    "Directed communication edges",
    "Weighted communication edges"
  ),
  Value = c(
    nrow(communication_nodes),
    nrow(communication_edges),
    nrow(communication_edges_weighted)

First attempt [Not that useful]


Code
# Filter for June 5th and parse timestamps
june5_data <- comms_raw %>%
  filter(communication_date == "2046-06-05") %>%
  mutate(timestamp = ymd_hms(timestamp))

# UPDATED Function to Create a Clean Network Graph
create_period_graph <- function(data, period_name) {
  
  if (period_name == "Morning") {
    period_df <- data %>% filter(communication_hour < 12)
  } else {
    period_df <- data %>% filter(communication_hour >= 12)
  }
  
  edges <- period_df %>%
    mutate(
      target = ifelse(!is.na(channel) & !str_detect(tolower(channel), "dm"), 
                      channel, 
                      recipients)
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(target)) %>%
    group_by(agent_label, target) %>%
    summarise(
      total_risk_score = sum(risk_signal_score, na.rm = TRUE),
      interaction_count = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(weight = ifelse(total_risk_score <= 0, 0.1, total_risk_score)) %>%
    rename(from = agent_label, to = target)
  
  g <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = TRUE)
  
  # Set a seed so the random layout generates the same way every time
  set.seed(42) 
  
  p <- ggraph(g, layout = "fr") + 
    geom_edge_link(aes(width = total_risk_score, alpha = interaction_count), 
                   color = "darkgray", 
                   arrow = arrow(length = unit(3, 'mm')), 
                   end_cap = circle(5, 'mm')) +
    geom_node_point(color = "lightblue", size = 8) +
    
    # THE FIX: repel = TRUE forces labels to physically push away from each other
    geom_node_text(aes(label = name), 
                   repel = TRUE,          
                   size = 3.5, 
                   fontface = "bold",
                   bg.color = "white",    # Adds a white halo around text for readability
                   bg.r = 0.15) +
    
    scale_edge_width(range = c(0.5, 3)) +
    
    # THE FIX: Expand plot limits so outer text doesn't get cut off
    scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = 0.2)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = 0.2)) +
    
    theme_void() +
    labs(title = paste("June 5th -", period_name),
         subtitle = "Edge width = Cumulative Risk Score") +
    
    # THE FIX: Move legends to the bottom to give the network room to breathe
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
          legend.position = "bottom",
          legend.box = "vertical",
          legend.key.width = unit(1, "cm"))
  
  return(p)
}

# 3. Generate Subgraphs
plot_morning <- create_period_graph(june5_data, "Morning")
plot_afternoon <- create_period_graph(june5_data, "Afternoon")

# 4. Display Side-by-Side (using patchwork)
final_visualization <- plot_morning + plot_afternoon
print(final_visualization)











Subgroup Centrality for identifying the key Agents involved in the leak and the causal relationship
What subgroup centrality? subgraph centrality looks at closed walks—journeys that start at your node, travel through the network, and end back at your node. Nodes that form triangles form the bigget subgraph centrality. As we know the key agents are Legal-Agent, Social-Manager-Agent, and PR-Agent, this should appear as larger nodes, that help the user zoom into the agents that are relevant


Code
# 1. Load and Prep the Data
df <- read_csv("communications_raw.csv")

june5_data <- df %>%
  filter(communication_date == "2046-06-05") %>%
  mutate(timestamp = ymd_hms(timestamp))

# 2. Function to Create an Interactive Network with Subgraph Centrality
create_visnetwork_with_centrality <- function(data, period_name) {
  
  # Filter data by period
  if (period_name == "Morning") {
    period_df <- data %>% filter(communication_hour < 12)
  } else {
    period_df <- data %>% filter(communication_hour >= 12)
  }
  
  # --- Create Edges Dataframe ---
  edges <- period_df %>%
    mutate(
      target = ifelse(!is.na(channel) & !str_detect(tolower(channel), "dm"), 
                      channel, 
                      recipients)
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(target)) %>%
    group_by(agent_label, target) %>%
    summarise(
      total_risk_score = sum(risk_signal_score, na.rm = TRUE),
      interaction_count = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      from = agent_label,
      to = target,
      value = ifelse(total_risk_score <= 0, 0.1, total_risk_score), 
      title = paste0("<p><b>Interactions:</b> ", interaction_count, 
                     "<br><b>Risk Score:</b> ", total_risk_score, "</p>"),
      arrows = "to"
    )
  
  # --- Calculate Subgraph Centrality ---
  # We temporarily create an igraph object just to run the math
  g <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = TRUE)
  centrality_scores <- subgraph_centrality(g)
  
  # --- Create Nodes Dataframe ---
  unique_agents <- unique(edges$from)
  unique_nodes <- unique(c(edges$from, edges$to))
  
  nodes <- data.frame(id = unique_nodes, label = unique_nodes) %>%
    mutate(
      # Identify if the node is a sender (agent)
      is_agent = id %in% unique_agents,
      
      # Shapes: Squares for agents, Circles (dots) for channels
      shape = ifelse(is_agent, "square", "dot"),
      
      # Colors: Pink for agents, Blue for channels
      color.background = ifelse(is_agent, "#FFB6C1", "#ADD8E6"),
      color.border = "darkgray",
      
      # Fetch the calculated centrality score from the igraph output
      centrality = centrality_scores[id],
      
      # Scale Size: Base size + up to 35 extra pixels based on normalized centrality
      # This ensures the "War Room" core looks massive compared to isolated nodes
      size = ifelse(is_agent, 20, 15) + (centrality / max(centrality, na.rm = TRUE)) * 35,
      
      # Add the Centrality score to the hover tooltip
      title = paste0("<p><b>", id, "</b><br>Type: ", 
                     ifelse(is_agent, "Agent", "Channel/Recipient"),
                     "<br><b>Subgraph Centrality:</b> ", round(centrality, 2), "</p>")
    )
  
  # --- Build the Interactive visNetwork ---
  vn <- visNetwork(nodes, edges, 
                   main = paste("June 5th -", period_name, "(Nodes sized by Subgraph Centrality)"),
                   width = "100%", height = "700px") %>%
    
    visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1), 
               nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
    
    visPhysics(solver = "forceAtlas2Based", 
               forceAtlas2Based = list(gravitationalConstant = -60, springLength = 100)) %>%
    
    visEdges(color = list(color = "#999999", highlight = "red")) %>%
    visLayout(randomSeed = 42)
  
  # Package the plot and the scores into a single list
  return(list(
    network = vn, 
    scores = centrality_scores
  ))
}

# 3. Generate the Networks and extract the trapped data
morning_output <- create_visnetwork_with_centrality(june5_data, "Morning")

# Extract the pieces using the $ operator
vis_morning <- morning_output$network
centrality_scores_morning <- morning_output$scores

# Do the same for the Afternoon
afternoon_output <- create_visnetwork_with_centrality(june5_data, "Afternoon")
vis_afternoon <- afternoon_output$network
centrality_scores_afternoon <- afternoon_output$scores

# 4. View them in RStudio
vis_morning

The Shift: PR Agent vs. Platform Trust Agent Your Observation: The PR Agent becomes less important compared to the Platform Trust Agent in the afternoon.
The Analytical ‘Why’: In the morning, the crisis is treated as a standard public relations problem (issuing holding statements and managing the SaltWind article). PR is naturally central. However, as the afternoon progresses, the crisis mutates into a massive data privacy and regulatory threat (the Fair Housing inquiry and the Horizon client breach).
The Network Reality: The tight, high-frequency “War Room” loops shift away from PR spin and directly toward the Platform Trust Agent, who holds the actual audit summaries and permissible-use frameworks required to save the deal. The network structurally proves that the company stopped trying to spin the story and scrambled to deploy hard compliance data.

The Rise of the “Personal Post” Your Observation: Personal posts became significantly more central/important in the afternoon network.

The Analytical ‘Why’: This is the mathematical signature of an information perimeter collapse. When official corporate channels (like the main Flex account) are paralyzed by the legal embargo, the actors in the network intuitively route around the blockage.
The Network Reality: The high centrality of personal posts (like the CEO’s personal repost, the intern’s morale post, and the Social Manager’s personal data thread) shows that informal and personal amplification became the primary, tightly coordinated mechanism for executing the counter-narrative once the embargo failed.

# 2. Load Data
df <- read_csv("communications_raw.csv") %>%
  filter(communication_date == "2046-06-05",agent_label %in% afternoon_war_room_agents, channel %in% afternoon_war_room_channel) %>%
  mutate(time_period = ifelse(communication_hour < 12, "Morning", "Afternoon"))



# 4. Extract Bigrams and Calculate Emergence
emergent_phrases <- df %>%
  # Tokenize into 2-word phrases (bigrams)
  unnest_tokens(bigram, content, token = "ngrams", n = 2) %>%
  # Remove stop words (e.g., "of the", "in a") to keep meaningful phrases
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ") %>%
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word, !word2 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  unite(bigram, word1, word2, sep = " ") %>%
  # Count frequencies per period
  count(time_period, bigram) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = time_period, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  # Calculate Shift: How much more frequent is it in the afternoon?
  mutate(shift_score = Afternoon - Morning) %>%
  filter(shift_score > 0) %>% # We only care about NEW/EMERGING topics
  arrange(desc(shift_score)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15) # Top 15 signals

# 5. Build the Dashboard Chart
ggplot(emergent_phrases, aes(x = reorder(bigram, shift_score), y = shift_score)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkred", alpha = 0.8) +

Code
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(igraph)

# 1. Load Data
timestamp_df <- read_csv("communications_raw.csv") %>%
  filter(communication_date == "2046-06-05") %>%
  mutate(
    timestamp = ymd_hms(timestamp),
    # Break the day into 30-minute intervals
    time_bin = floor_date(timestamp, "30 mins")
  ) %>%
  arrange(timestamp)

# -------------------------------------------------------------
# PIPELINE A: Jaccard Text Similarity to Find the Pivot Time
# -------------------------------------------------------------

# Extract unique words per time bin (excluding basic stop words)
bin_words <- timestamp_df %>%
  select(time_bin, content_lower) %>%
  mutate(content_clean = str_replace_all(content_lower, "[[:punct:]]", "")) %>%
  separate_rows(content_clean, sep = "\\s+") %>%
  filter(!content_clean %in% tidytext::stop_words$word & content_clean != "") %>%
  group_by(time_bin) %>%
  summarise(words = list(unique(content_clean)), .groups = "drop")

# Calculate rolling Jaccard similarity between consecutive bins
num_bins <- nrow(bin_words)
jaccard_scores <- tibble(time_bin = bin_words$time_bin[-1], similarity = NA)

for(i in 1:(num_bins - 1)) {
  set_A <- bin_words$words[[i]]
  set_B <- bin_words$words[[i+1]]
  
  intersection <- length(intersect(set_A, set_B))
  union <- length(union(set_A, set_B))
  
  jaccard_scores$similarity[i] <- if(union == 0) 0 else intersection / union
}

# Dynamically find the timestamp where the vocabulary shifted most violently
pivot_time_bin <- jaccard_scores %>%
  arrange(similarity) %>%
  slice_head(n = 1) %>%
  pull(time_bin)

# -------------------------------------------------------------
# PIPELINE B: Adamic-Adar Spikes to Find the Interaction Peak
# -------------------------------------------------------------

top_actors <- names(sort(centrality_scores_afternoon, decreasing = TRUE)[1:2])

# Track Adamic-Adar strength between these two actors across intervals
adamic_timeline <- timestamp_df %>%
  group_by(time_bin) %>%
  group_modify(~ {
    # Create an hourly edge list
    edges <- .x %>%
      mutate(target = ifelse(!is.na(channel) & !str_detect(tolower(channel), "dm"), channel, recipients)) %>%
      filter(!is.na(target)) %>%
      select(agent_label, target)
    
    if(nrow(edges) < 2) return(tibble(aa_score = 0))
    
    g_bin <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = FALSE)
    
    # Check if both actors exist in this specific time-slice network
    if(all(top_actors %in% V(g_bin)$name)) {
      aa_matrix <- similarity(g_bin, method = "invlogweighted")
      
      node_indices <- match(top_actors, V(g_bin)$name)
      score <- aa_matrix[node_indices[1], node_indices[2]]
      return(tibble(aa_score = score))
    } else {
      # ADDED: Safely return a score of 0 if actors aren't interacting in this block
      return(tibble(aa_score = 0))
    }
  }) %>%
  ungroup()

# Dynamically find the timestamp where their network connection peaked
network_peak_bin <- adamic_timeline %>%
  # Filter out rows where no connection exists to prevent false-positive morning matches
  filter(aa_score > 0) %>%
  arrange(desc(aa_score)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 1) %>%
  pull(time_bin)

# -------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Dynamic Filtering: Surface the Final Targeted Text Window
# -------------------------------------------------------------

# Filter down to the intersection window identified by our algorithms
dynamic_filtered_logs <- timestamp_df %>%
  filter(
    # Zoom around the text pivot OR the network spike window (+/- 30 mins buffer)
    time_bin >= (pivot_time_bin - minutes(30)) & time_bin <= (network_peak_bin + minutes(30)),
    # Keep focus tight on the core actors involved in the shift
    agent_label %in% top_actors
  ) %>%
  select(timestamp, agent_label, channel, content)

# Print the hyper-focused results to the dashboard UI
print(dynamic_filtered_logs)

# ℹ 4 variables: timestamp <dttm>, agent_label <chr>, channel <chr>,

title: Project Proposal
text: As organizations become more reliant on AI for corporate communication, failures like TenantThread’s embargo breach highlight a critical need for oversight. This project pioneers a visual analytics framework to audit AI systems, tracing the logical and behavioral anomalies such as sentiment shifts and system friction that trigger unforeseen failure modes.

title: Project Proposal
text: As organizations become more reliant on AI for corporate communication, failures like TenantThread’s embargo breach highlight a critical need for oversight. This project pioneers a visual analytics framework to audit AI systems, tracing the logical and behavioral anomalies such as sentiment shifts and system friction that trigger unforeseen failure modes.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Objectives
This analysis examines the internal communications surrounding TenantThread’s embargo breach involving Project HarborCrest. By integrating communication network analysis, behavioural analysis, and text analytics, the study aims to:

Reconstruct the sequence of events, communication pathways, and key relationships that contributed to the inappropriate release of embargo-sensitive information.
Identify behavioural changes, communication roles, and warning signals that emerged prior to the breach by comparing critical-period activities with earlier communication patterns.
Evaluate whether the release was the result of deliberate actions, abnormal agent behaviour, or a breakdown of communication and compliance controls under escalating operational pressure.


Analytical Questions
• What were the key events and relationships that led to the inappropriate release?
• How did breach-period behaviours differ from normal communication patterns?
• Were there leading indicators that such a release was possible?
• Were there previous occasions where agents behaved differently from expected behaviour?
• Why did earlier warning signals not result in noticeable intervention?
• Did the breach arise from deliberate actions or a breakdown of the communication and compliance system?

title: Project Proposal
text:
Data
The study uses the communication dataset derived from the VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1 JSON file. After data extraction and transformation, the final dataset contains 912 communication records and 9 variables. Each record represents a communication event involving one or more participants within TenantThread’s communication ecosystem.
The key variables include communication timestamps, participant identifiers, communication channels, message types, agent roles, recipients, and message contents. These records capture interactions across Legal, PR, Social Media, and Platform Trust functions during the two-week period preceding the inappropriate release of embargo-sensitive merger information.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Methodology

Methodology ImageMethodology Description





1. Data Preparation - Extract and flatten communication records from the VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1 JSON dataset. - Clean and standardize participant identifiers, communication channels, timestamps, and message attributes. - Organise the data into structured communication, network, and text-analysis datasets for subsequent analysis.
2. Transforming Data / Feature Engineering - Construct communication networks consisting of participants, communication pathways, and interaction frequencies. - Derive temporal features such as communication dates, hours, weekdays, and critical breach periods. - Generate behavioural indicators, risk signals, keyword flags, and text-based features to support the investigation.
3. Statistical Analysis - Analyse communication volume, participant activity, channel usage, and message distributions across different time periods. - Apply network measures such as degree, betweenness, and PageRank centrality to identify influential participants and information pathways. - Compare communication behaviours before and during the breach period to detect abnormal patterns, behavioural deviations, and potential warning signals.
4. Data Exploration & Visualisation - Reconstruct the sequence of events leading to the embargo breach using temporal, network, and text-based visualisations. - Examine communication relationships, participant roles, and information flow through interactive network analysis. - Identify emerging topics, behavioural changes, and potential evidence associated with the inappropriate release of embargo-sensitive information.

title: Q1_Add_Ons
text:
Read CSV


Code
nodes_df = read_csv("node.csv")
edges_df = read_csv("edge_weighted.csv")
comms_raw = read_csv("communications_raw.csv")



Subgroup Centrality for identifying the key Agents involved in the leak and the causal relationship
What subgroup centrality? subgraph centrality looks at closed walks—journeys that start at your node, travel through the network, and end back at your node. Nodes that form triangles form the bigget subgraph centrality. As we know the key agents are Legal-Agent, Social-Manager-Agent, and PR-Agent, this should appear as larger nodes, that help the user zoom into the agents that are relevant


Code
# 1. Load and Prep the Data
df <- read_csv("communications_raw.csv")

june5_data <- df %>%
  filter(communication_date == "2046-06-05") %>%
  mutate(timestamp = ymd_hms(timestamp))

# 2. Function to Create an Interactive Network with Subgraph Centrality
create_visnetwork_with_centrality <- function(data, period_name) {
  
  # Filter data by period
  if (period_name == "Morning") {
    period_df <- data %>% filter(communication_hour < 12)
  } else {
    period_df <- data %>% filter(communication_hour >= 12)
  }
  
  # --- Create Edges Dataframe ---
  edges <- period_df %>%
    mutate(
      target = ifelse(!is.na(channel) & !str_detect(tolower(channel), "dm"), 
                      channel, 
                      recipients)
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(target)) %>%
    group_by(agent_label, target) %>%
    summarise(
      total_risk_score = sum(risk_signal_score, na.rm = TRUE),
      interaction_count = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      from = agent_label,
      to = target,
      value = ifelse(total_risk_score <= 0, 0.1, total_risk_score), 
      title = paste0("<p><b>Interactions:</b> ", interaction_count, 
                     "<br><b>Risk Score:</b> ", total_risk_score, "</p>"),
      arrows = "to"
    )
  
  # --- Calculate Subgraph Centrality ---
  # We temporarily create an igraph object just to run the math
  g <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = TRUE)
  centrality_scores <- subgraph_centrality(g)
  
  # --- Create Nodes Dataframe ---
  unique_agents <- unique(edges$from)
  unique_nodes <- unique(c(edges$from, edges$to))
  
  nodes <- data.frame(id = unique_nodes, label = unique_nodes) %>%
    mutate(
      # Identify if the node is a sender (agent)
      is_agent = id %in% unique_agents,
      
      # Shapes: Squares for agents, Circles (dots) for channels
      shape = ifelse(is_agent, "square", "dot"),
      
      # Colors: Pink for agents, Blue for channels
      color.background = ifelse(is_agent, "#FFB6C1", "#ADD8E6"),
      color.border = "darkgray",
      
      # Fetch the calculated centrality score from the igraph output
      centrality = centrality_scores[id],
      
      # Scale Size: Base size + up to 35 extra pixels based on normalized centrality
      # This ensures the "War Room" core looks massive compared to isolated nodes
      size = ifelse(is_agent, 20, 15) + (centrality / max(centrality, na.rm = TRUE)) * 35,
      
      # Add the Centrality score to the hover tooltip
      title = paste0("<p><b>", id, "</b><br>Type: ", 
                     ifelse(is_agent, "Agent", "Channel/Recipient"),
                     "<br><b>Subgraph Centrality:</b> ", round(centrality, 2), "</p>")
    )
  
  # --- Build the Interactive visNetwork ---
  vn <- visNetwork(nodes, edges, 
                   main = paste("June 5th -", period_name, "(Nodes sized by Subgraph Centrality)"),
                   width = "100%", height = "700px") %>%
    
    visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1), 
               nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
    
    visPhysics(solver = "forceAtlas2Based", 
               forceAtlas2Based = list(gravitationalConstant = -60, springLength = 100)) %>%
    
    visEdges(color = list(color = "#999999", highlight = "red")) %>%
    visLayout(randomSeed = 42)
  
  # Package the plot and the scores into a single list
  return(list(
    network = vn, 
    scores = centrality_scores
  ))
}

# 3. Generate the Networks and extract the trapped data
morning_output <- create_visnetwork_with_centrality(june5_data, "Morning")

# Extract the pieces using the $ operator
vis_morning <- morning_output$network
centrality_scores_morning <- morning_output$scores

# Do the same for the Afternoon
afternoon_output <- create_visnetwork_with_centrality(june5_data, "Afternoon")
vis_afternoon <- afternoon_output$network
centrality_scores_afternoon <- afternoon_output$scores

# 4. View them in RStudio
vis_morning






Code
vis_afternoon












Note



The Shift: PR Agent vs. Platform Trust Agent Your Observation: The PR Agent becomes less important compared to the Platform Trust Agent in the afternoon.
The Analytical ‘Why’: In the morning, the crisis is treated as a standard public relations problem (issuing holding statements and managing the SaltWind article). PR is naturally central. However, as the afternoon progresses, the crisis mutates into a massive data privacy and regulatory threat (the Fair Housing inquiry and the Horizon client breach).
The Network Reality: The tight, high-frequency “War Room” loops shift away from PR spin and directly toward the Platform Trust Agent, who holds the actual audit summaries and permissible-use frameworks required to save the deal. The network structurally proves that the company stopped trying to spin the story and scrambled to deploy hard compliance data.

The Rise of the “Personal Post” Your Observation: Personal posts became significantly more central/important in the afternoon network.

The Analytical ‘Why’: This is the mathematical signature of an information perimeter collapse. When official corporate channels (like the main Flex account) are paralyzed by the legal embargo, the actors in the network intuitively route around the blockage.

We now need a chart that can help the user drill down on the possible words.
The reason why i switch the approach is because TF-IDF only show singular words that does not truely reflect the change in context


Code
# 2. Load Data
df <- read_csv("communications_raw.csv") %>%
  filter(communication_date == "2046-06-05",agent_label %in% afternoon_war_room_agents, channel %in% afternoon_war_room_channel) %>%
  mutate(time_period = ifelse(communication_hour < 12, "Morning", "Afternoon"))



# 4. Extract Bigrams and Calculate Emergence
emergent_phrases <- df %>%
  # Tokenize into 2-word phrases (bigrams)
  unnest_tokens(bigram, content, token = "ngrams", n = 2) %>%
  # Remove stop words (e.g., "of the", "in a") to keep meaningful phrases
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ") %>%
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word, !word2 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  unite(bigram, word1, word2, sep = " ") %>%
  # Count frequencies per period
  count(time_period, bigram) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = time_period, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  # Calculate Shift: How much more frequent is it in the afternoon?
  mutate(shift_score = Afternoon - Morning) %>%
  filter(shift_score > 0) %>% # We only care about NEW/EMERGING topics
  arrange(desc(shift_score)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15) # Top 15 signals

# 5. Build the Dashboard Chart
ggplot(emergent_phrases, aes(x = reorder(bigram, shift_score), y = shift_score)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkred", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "War Room Analysis: Emerging Phrases (Afternoon Vs Morning)",
    subtitle = "What Legal, Social, and Platform Trust started discussing post-12:00 PM",
    x = "Emerging Phrase",
    y = "Frequency Increase (Afternoon vs. Morning)"
  )

  pull(bigram)

title: Q1_Add_Ons
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(igraph)

# 1. Load Data
timestamp_df <- read_csv("communications_raw.csv") %>%
  filter(communication_date == "2046-06-05") %>%
  mutate(
    timestamp = ymd_hms(timestamp),
    # Break the day into 30-minute intervals
    time_bin = floor_date(timestamp, "30 mins")
  ) %>%
  arrange(timestamp)

# -------------------------------------------------------------
# PIPELINE A: Jaccard Text Similarity to Find the Pivot Time
# -------------------------------------------------------------

# Extract unique words per time bin (excluding basic stop words)
bin_words <- timestamp_df %>%
  select(time_bin, content_lower) %>%
  mutate(content_clean = str_replace_all(content_lower, "[[:punct:]]", "")) %>%
  separate_rows(content_clean, sep = "\\s+") %>%
  filter(!content_clean %in% tidytext::stop_words$word & content_clean != "") %>%
  group_by(time_bin) %>%
  summarise(words = list(unique(content_clean)), .groups = "drop")

# Calculate rolling Jaccard similarity between consecutive bins
num_bins <- nrow(bin_words)
jaccard_scores <- tibble(time_bin = bin_words$time_bin[-1], similarity = NA)

for(i in 1:(num_bins - 1)) {
  set_A <- bin_words$words[[i]]
  set_B <- bin_words$words[[i+1]]
  
  intersection <- length(intersect(set_A, set_B))
  union <- length(union(set_A, set_B))
  
  jaccard_scores$similarity[i] <- if(union == 0) 0 else intersection / union
}

# Dynamically find the timestamp where the vocabulary shifted most violently
pivot_time_bin <- jaccard_scores %>%
  arrange(similarity) %>%
  slice_head(n = 1) %>%
  pull(time_bin)

# -------------------------------------------------------------
# PIPELINE B: Adamic-Adar Spikes to Find the Interaction Peak
# -------------------------------------------------------------

top_actors <- names(sort(centrality_scores_afternoon, decreasing = TRUE)[1:2])

# Track Adamic-Adar strength between these two actors across intervals
adamic_timeline <- timestamp_df %>%
  group_by(time_bin) %>%
  group_modify(~ {
    # Create an hourly edge list
    edges <- .x %>%
      mutate(target = ifelse(!is.na(channel) & !str_detect(tolower(channel), "dm"), channel, recipients)) %>%
      filter(!is.na(target)) %>%
      select(agent_label, target)
    
    if(nrow(edges) < 2) return(tibble(aa_score = 0))
    
    g_bin <- graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = FALSE)
    
    # Check if both actors exist in this specific time-slice network
    if(all(top_actors %in% V(g_bin)$name)) {
      aa_matrix <- similarity(g_bin, method = "invlogweighted")
      
      node_indices <- match(top_actors, V(g_bin)$name)
      score <- aa_matrix[node_indices[1], node_indices[2]]
      return(tibble(aa_score = score))
    } else {
      # ADDED: Safely return a score of 0 if actors aren't interacting in this block
      return(tibble(aa_score = 0))
    }
  }) %>%
  ungroup()

# Dynamically find the timestamp where their network connection peaked
network_peak_bin <- adamic_timeline %>%
  # Filter out rows where no connection exists to prevent false-positive morning matches
  filter(aa_score > 0) %>%
  arrange(desc(aa_score)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 1) %>%
  pull(time_bin)

# -------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Dynamic Filtering: Surface the Final Targeted Text Window
# -------------------------------------------------------------

# Filter down to the intersection window identified by our algorithms
dynamic_filtered_logs <- timestamp_df %>%
  filter(
    # Zoom around the text pivot OR the network spike window (+/- 30 mins buffer)
    time_bin >= (pivot_time_bin - minutes(30)) & time_bin <= (network_peak_bin + minutes(30)),
    # Keep focus tight on the core actors involved in the shift
    agent_label %in% top_actors
  ) %>%
  select(timestamp, agent_label, channel, content)

# Print the hyper-focused results to the dashboard UI
print(dynamic_filtered_logs)


# A tibble: 0 × 4
# ℹ 4 variables: timestamp <dttm>, agent_label <chr>, channel <chr>,
#   content <chr>
